title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: onet2r package provides modern R interface O*NET Web Services API 2.0. vignette guide basics using package search occupations, retrieve occupation details, work O*NET database tables.

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: can use onet2r, need obtain configure API key O*NET.

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: Visit O*NET Web Services registration page Fill registration form ’ll receive API key via email

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: two ways set API key: Option 1: Set current R session Option 2: Set permanently .Renviron file (recommended) Verify API key set: see error, make sure ’ve set environment variable correctly restarted R.
code: Sys.setenv(ONET_API_KEY = "your-api-key-here") # Open your .Renviron file usethis::edit_r_environ()  # Add this line and save: # ONET_API_KEY=your-api-key-here  # Restart R for changes to take effect library(onet2r)  # This should return your API key onet_api_key()

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: common starting point searching occupations keyword: returns tibble occupation codes titles. can control number results:
code: # Search for software development roles software_jobs <- onet_search("software developer") software_jobs # Get more results (up to 100) onet_search("engineer", start = 1, end = 100)  # Get a specific range of results onet_search("nurse", start = 11, end = 30)

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: can also search using O*NET-SOC occupation codes:
code: # Search by code onet_search("15-1252")  # Or with full code including detail level onet_search("15-1252.00")

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: occupation code, can retrieve detailed information:

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: Get comprehensive summary occupation:
code: # Get summary for Software Developers summary <- onet_occupation("15-1252.00")  # The summary is a list containing various occupation attributes names(summary)

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: see detail sections available occupation:
code: details <- onet_occupation_details("15-1252.00")  # This includes links to detail sections such as skills and tasks str(details, max.level = 1)

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: Retrieve specific occupation attributes tibbles: returns tibble : - id: Element identifier - name: Name skill/knowledge/ability - description: Detailed description - value: Importance level rating - scale: scale type (importance/level)
code: # Get skills required for the occupation skills <- onet_skills("15-1252.00") head(skills)  # Get knowledge areas knowledge <- onet_knowledge("15-1252.00") head(knowledge)  # Get abilities abilities <- onet_abilities("15-1252.00") head(abilities)

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: Find hot technologies associated occupation:
code: tech <- onet_technology("15-1252.00") tech  # Technologies are organized by category library(dplyr) tech |>   count(category, sort = TRUE)

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: Get complete list occupations O*NET database:
code: # Get all occupations (may return 1000+ results) all_occupations <- onet_occupations() nrow(all_occupations)  # Get a subset first_50 <- onet_occupations(start = 1, end = 50) first_50

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: Combine search detail retrieval analyze patterns:
code: library(purrr) library(dplyr)  # Find data-related occupations data_jobs <- onet_search("data", start = 1, end = 10)  # Get skills for each occupation skills_by_job <- data_jobs$code |>   map(possibly(onet_skills, otherwise = tibble())) |>   set_names(data_jobs$title) |>   list_rbind(names_to = "occupation")  # Find most common skills across these occupations top_skills <- skills_by_job |>   count(name, sort = TRUE) |>   head(10)  top_skills

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: O*NET database contains many detailed tables can access directly.

title: Getting Started with onet2r
code: tables <- onet_tables() tables  # Find tables of interest tables |>   filter(grepl("skill", title, ignore.case = TRUE))

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: retrieving table, can check structure:
code: # Get column information occupation_cols <- onet_table_info("occupation_data") occupation_cols

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: Retrieve rows table automatic pagination: onet_table() function: - Automatically handles pagination large tables - Fetches 2000 rows per request (API maximum) - Shows progress multi-page retrievals - Returns single combined tibble
code: # Get the entire skills table # This automatically paginates through all results skills_table <- onet_table("skills")  # Progress messages are shown by default: # Fetching rows 2001 to 4000 of 12345...  # Disable progress messages if desired skills_quiet <- onet_table("skills", show_progress = FALSE)

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: large tables, can adjust page size:
code: # Fetch in smaller chunks occupation_data <- onet_table("occupation_data", page_size = 500)

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: Find civilian occupations match military job titles codes:
code: # Search by military job title civilian_roles <- onet_crosswalk_military("infantry") civilian_roles  # Search by military occupational specialty (MOS) code onet_crosswalk_military("11B")

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: Map occupation codes O*NET-SOC taxonomy versions:
code: # Map from current O*NET-SOC to 2010 SOC mapped_2010 <- onet_taxonomy_map(   "15-1252.00",   from = "active",   to = "2010" ) mapped_2010  # Map from 2010 SOC to current O*NET-SOC mapped_current <- onet_taxonomy_map(   "15-1131.00",   from = "2010",   to = "active" ) mapped_current

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: package handles errors gracefully provides informative messages:

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: Missing API Key: Invalid Occupation Code:
code: # If no API key is set onet_search("engineer") #> Error: O*NET API key not found. #> <U+2139> Set your API key with `Sys.setenv(ONET_API_KEY = "your-key")` #> <U+2139> Get a key at <https://services.onetcenter.org/developer/> onet_skills("invalid-code") #> Error: Invalid O*NET-SOC code format: "invalid-code" #> <U+2139> Expected format: XX-XXXX or XX-XXXX.XX (e.g., 15-1252 or 15-1252.00)

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: searches lookups return results, get empty tibble correct schema:
code: # Search for something that doesn't exist results <- onet_search("xyznonexistent123") #> No occupations found for keyword: "xyznonexistent123"  # Still returns a properly structured tibble results #> # A tibble: 0 <U+00D7> 2 #> # <U+2139> 2 variables: code <chr>, title <chr>  # You can safely use it in pipelines nrow(results) #> [1] 0

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: package automatically handles transient API errors: Rate limits (HTTP 429): Automatically retries exponential backoff Server errors (500, 502, 503, 504): Retries 3 times API errors: Surfaces clear error messages O*NET API
code: # If the API returns an error, you'll see a descriptive message: #> Error: O*NET API returned an error: #> <U+2716> Invalid table identifier #> <U+2139> Use onet_tables() to see available tables

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: onet2r functions return tibbles work seamlessly tidyverse tools:
code: library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(ggplot2)  # Find healthcare occupations and analyze skills healthcare <- onet_search("healthcare", start = 1, end = 20)  # Get skills for top 5 occupations skills <- healthcare |>   slice_head(n = 5) |>   pull(code) |>   map(onet_skills) |>   list_rbind(.id = "occupation_num")  # Add occupation titles skills_with_titles <- skills |>   mutate(occupation_num = as.integer(occupation_num)) |>   left_join(     healthcare |>       slice_head(n = 5) |>       mutate(occupation_num = row_number()),     by = "occupation_num"   )  # Find top skills across these occupations top_skills <- skills_with_titles |>   filter(scale == "Importance") |>   group_by(name) |>   summarise(     avg_importance = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE),     n_occupations = n()   ) |>   arrange(desc(avg_importance)) |>   head(10)  top_skills

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: Since API calls consume rate limit, consider caching results:
code: # Save results for later use occupations <- onet_occupations() saveRDS(occupations, "data/onet_occupations.rds")  # Load cached results cached_occupations <- readRDS("data/onet_occupations.rds")

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: retrieving details multiple occupations, handle errors gracefully:
code: library(purrr)  codes <- c("15-1252.00", "15-1251.00", "invalid-code", "29-1141.00")  # Use possibly() to handle errors safe_skills <- possibly(onet_skills, otherwise = NULL)  skills_list <- codes |>   map(safe_skills) |>   compact() # Remove NULL entries  # Or keep track of which ones failed results <- codes |>   map(safely(onet_skills))  # Extract successful results successful <- results |>   map("result") |>   compact()  # Check for errors errors <- results |>   map("error") |>   compact()

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: mindful API rate limits, especially processing many requests:
code: # Add delays between requests if needed occupations <- c("15-1252.00", "15-1251.00", "29-1141.00")  skills_list <- occupations |>   map(\(code) {     result <- onet_skills(code)     Sys.sleep(0.5) # Wait 0.5 seconds between requests     result   })

title: Getting Started with onet2r
text: vignette covered: Setting API authentication Searching occupations Retrieving occupation details (skills, knowledge, abilities, technology) Working database tables Using crosswalks taxonomy mapping Error handling best practices Integrating tidyverse workflows onet2r package provides comprehensive, user-friendly interface O*NET Web Services API, making easy incorporate occupational data R analyses.

title: Adding OEWS wage and employment context
text: parser normalizes column names snake_case converts OEWS numeric fields employment annual median wages formatted strings numeric columns.
code: oews <- onet_oews_national(year = 2024, path = sample_oews) oews #> # A tibble: 3 × 7 #>    year oews_type occ_code occ_title           tot_emp a_median h_median #>   <int> <chr>     <chr>    <chr>                 <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1  2024 national  15-1252  Software Developers 1847900   133080     64.0 #> 2  2024 national  29-1141  Registered Nurses   3175400    93070     44.8 #> 3  2024 national  11-1011  Chief Executives     211230   206680     99.4 str(oews) #> tibble [3 × 7] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame) #>  $ year     : int [1:3] 2024 2024 2024 #>  $ oews_type: chr [1:3] "national" "national" "national" #>  $ occ_code : chr [1:3] "15-1252" "29-1141" "11-1011" #>  $ occ_title: chr [1:3] "Software Developers" "Registered Nurses" "Chief Executives" #>  $ tot_emp  : num [1:3] 1847900 3175400 211230 #>  $ a_median : num [1:3] 133080 93070 206680 #>  $ h_median : num [1:3] 64 44.8 99.4

what: Join OEWS to O*NET occupation codes
title: Adding OEWS wage and employment context
text: O*NET-SOC codes commonly include detail suffix, .00. OEWS uses six-digit SOC codes. onet_join_oews() creates soc_code column joins normalized code.
code: occupations <- tibble::tibble(   code = c("15-1252.00", "29-1141.00", "11-1011.00"),   title = c("Software Developers", "Registered Nurses", "Chief Executives") )  occupation_wages <- onet_join_oews(occupations, oews = oews) occupation_wages #> # A tibble: 3 × 9 #>   code       title  soc_code  year oews_type occ_title tot_emp a_median h_median #>   <chr>      <chr>  <chr>    <int> <chr>     <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 15-1252.00 Softw… 15-1252   2024 national  Software… 1847900   133080     64.0 #> 2 29-1141.00 Regis… 29-1141   2024 national  Register… 3175400    93070     44.8 #> 3 11-1011.00 Chief… 11-1011   2024 national  Chief Ex…  211230   206680     99.4

title: Adding OEWS wage and employment context
text: Many research workflows need turn occupation-level O*NET records labor-market-weighted measures. example, skill can appear multiple occupations different skill ratings different employment levels. output includes: n_records: number rows used grouped summary n_occupations: number occupations contributing summary total_weight: sum employment weights weighted_mean: employment-weighted mean selected O*NET value wage_weighted_mean: mean weighted employment multiplied annual median wage
code: skill_rows <- tibble::tibble(   code = c("15-1252.00", "15-1252.00", "29-1141.00", "29-1141.00"),   element_id = c("2.A.1.a", "2.A.1.b", "2.A.1.a", "2.A.1.b"),   element_name = c(     "Reading Comprehension",     "Active Listening",     "Reading Comprehension",     "Active Listening"   ),   data_value = c(4.12, 4.00, 3.88, 4.25) )  onet_weighted_summary(   skill_rows,   group = c("element_id", "element_name"),   value = "data_value",   oews = oews ) #> # A tibble: 2 × 7 #>   element_id element_name     n_records n_occupations total_weight weighted_mean #>   <chr>      <chr>                <int>         <int>        <dbl>         <dbl> #> 1 2.A.1.a    Reading Compreh…         2             2      5023300          3.97 #> 2 2.A.1.b    Active Listening         2             2      5023300          4.16 #> # ℹ 1 more variable: wage_weighted_mean <dbl>

title: Adding OEWS wage and employment context
text: workflow uses ACS CPS microdata instead OEWS employment counts, onet_pums_employment_weights() converts individual records SOC-level weights. SOCP PWGTP names match common ACS PUMS output tidycensus::get_pums(). live ACS pulls, start tidycensus pass resulting data frame onet_pums_employment_weights():
code: pums <- tibble::tibble(   SOCP = c("151252", "151252", "291141"),   PWGTP = c(120, 80, 200) )  onet_pums_employment_weights(pums) #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   soc_code employment records #>   <chr>         <dbl>   <int> #> 1 15-1252         200       2 #> 2 29-1141         200       1 pums <- tidycensus::get_pums(   variables = c("SOCP", "PWGTP", "WAGP"),   state = "all",   survey = "acs1",   year = 2023 )  weights <- onet_pums_employment_weights(pums)

title: Adding OEWS wage and employment context
text: BLS publishes annual public downloads. calls download cache corresponding file: automated downloads rate-limited, download ZIP manually BLS OEWS public data pages pass local path:
code: national <- onet_oews_national(2024) states <- onet_oews_state(2024) metros <- onet_oews_metro(2024) industries <- onet_oews_industry(2024) national <- onet_oews_national(2024, path = "oesm24nat.zip")

title: Validating onet2r outputs
text: validation script always checks deterministic local outputs: reading bundled OEWS sample data joining O*NET occupation codes OEWS estimates computing employment- wage-weighted O*NET summaries configuring cache rate-limit settings ONET_API_KEY available, also performs live smoke checks across main exported O*NET functions. result inspected expected type, minimum row count, important columns.

what: Example: local validation without an API key
title: Validating onet2r outputs
code: library(onet2r)  sample_oews <- system.file(   "extdata",   "oews-national-sample.csv",   package = "onet2r" )  oews <- onet_oews_national(2024, path = sample_oews) stopifnot(inherits(oews, "tbl_df")) stopifnot(all(c("occ_code", "tot_emp", "a_median") %in% names(oews)))  occupations <- tibble::tibble(   code = c("15-1252.00", "29-1141.00"),   title = c("Software Developers", "Registered Nurses") )  joined <- onet_join_oews(occupations, oews = oews) stopifnot(all(c("soc_code", "tot_emp", "a_median") %in% names(joined))) joined #> # A tibble: 2 × 9 #>   code       title  soc_code  year oews_type occ_title tot_emp a_median h_median #>   <chr>      <chr>  <chr>    <int> <chr>     <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 15-1252.00 Softw… 15-1252   2024 national  Software… 1847900   133080     64.0 #> 2 29-1141.00 Regis… 29-1141   2024 national  Register… 3175400    93070     44.8

what: Example: live validation with an API key
title: Validating onet2r outputs
text: goal replace unit tests. give maintainers users repeatable, practical way confirm live API outputs still match shapes used examples, vignettes, downstream analyses.
code: Sys.getenv("ONET_API_KEY")  search_results <- onet_search("software developer", end = 5) stopifnot(inherits(search_results, "tbl_df")) stopifnot(all(c("code", "title") %in% names(search_results))) stopifnot(nrow(search_results) > 0)  skills <- onet_skills("15-1252.00", end = 5) stopifnot(inherits(skills, "tbl_df")) stopifnot(nrow(skills) > 0)  job_zone <- onet_job_zone("15-1252.00") stopifnot(is.list(job_zone))

title: Modern R Development Guide
text: R package wrapping O*NET Web Services API 2.0. API Details: - Base URL: https://api-v2.onetcenter.org - Auth: X-API-Key header - Format: JSON responses - Docs: https://services.onetcenter.org/reference/

title: Modern R Development Guide
text: Use modern tidyverse patterns - Prioritize dplyr 1.1+ features, native pipe, current APIs Profile optimizing - Use profvis bench identify real bottlenecks Write readable code first - Optimize necessary profiling Follow tidyverse style guide - Consistent naming, spacing, structure

title: Modern R Development Guide
text: Always use native pipe |> instead magrittr %>% R 4.3+ provides needed features
code: # Good - Modern native pipe data |>    filter(year >= 2020) |>   summarise(mean_value = mean(value))  # Avoid - Legacy magrittr pipe   data %>%    filter(year >= 2020) %>%   summarise(mean_value = mean(value))

title: Modern R Development Guide
text: Use join_by() instead character vectors joins Support inequality, rolling, overlap joins
code: # Good - Modern join syntax transactions |>    inner_join(companies, by = join_by(company == id))  # Good - Inequality joins transactions |>   inner_join(companies, join_by(company == id, year >= since))  # Good - Rolling joins (closest match) transactions |>   inner_join(companies, join_by(company == id, closest(year >= since)))  # Avoid - Old character vector syntax transactions |>    inner_join(companies, by = c("company" = "id"))

title: Modern R Development Guide
text: Use multiple unmatched arguments quality control
code: # Expect 1:1 matches, error on multiple inner_join(x, y, by = join_by(id), multiple = "error")  # Allow multiple matches explicitly   inner_join(x, y, by = join_by(id), multiple = "all")  # Ensure all rows match inner_join(x, y, by = join_by(id), unmatched = "error")

title: Modern R Development Guide
text: Understand difference data masking tidy selection Use {{}} (embrace) function arguments Use .data[[]] character vectors
code: # Data masking functions: arrange(), filter(), mutate(), summarise() # Tidy selection functions: select(), relocate(), across()  # Function arguments - embrace with {{}} my_summary <- function(data, group_var, summary_var) {   data |>     group_by({{ group_var }}) |>     summarise(mean_val = mean({{ summary_var }})) }  # Character vectors - use .data[[]] for (var in names(mtcars)) {   mtcars |> count(.data[[var]]) |> print() }  # Multiple columns - use across() data |>    summarise(across({{ summary_vars }}, ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)))

title: Modern R Development Guide
text: Use .per-operation grouping (dplyr 1.1+) Use pick() column selection inside data-masking functions Use across() applying functions multiple columns Use reframe() multi-row summaries
code: # Good - Per-operation grouping (always returns ungrouped) data |>   summarise(mean_value = mean(value), .by = category)  # Good - Multiple grouping variables data |>   summarise(total = sum(revenue), .by = c(company, year))  # Good - pick() for column selection data |>   summarise(     n_x_cols = ncol(pick(starts_with("x"))),     n_y_cols = ncol(pick(starts_with("y")))   )  # Good - across() for applying functions data |>   summarise(across(where(is.numeric), mean, .names = "mean_{.col}"), .by = group)  # Good - reframe() for multi-row results data |>   reframe(quantiles = quantile(x, c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75)), .by = group)  # Avoid - Old persistent grouping pattern data |>   group_by(category) |>   summarise(mean_value = mean(value)) |>   ungroup()

title: Modern R Development Guide
text: Data-masking allows R expressions refer data frame columns variables environment. rlang provides metaprogramming framework powers tidyverse data-masking.

previous_headings: Modern rlang Patterns for Data-Masking > Core Concepts
title: Modern R Development Guide
text: Embracing {{}} - Forward function arguments data-masking functions Injection !! - Inject single expressions values Splicing !!! - Inject multiple arguments list Dynamic dots - Programmable ... injection support Pronouns .data/.env - Explicit disambiguation data environment variables

previous_headings: Modern rlang Patterns for Data-Masking > Function Argument Patterns
title: Modern R Development Guide
text: Use {{}} forward function arguments data-masking functions:
code: # Single argument forwarding my_summarise <- function(data, var) {   data |> dplyr::summarise(mean = mean({{ var }})) }  # Works with any data-masking expression mtcars |> my_summarise(cyl) mtcars |> my_summarise(cyl * am) mtcars |> my_summarise(.data$cyl)  # pronoun syntax supported

previous_headings: Modern rlang Patterns for Data-Masking > Function Argument Patterns
title: Modern R Development Guide
code: # Simple dots forwarding my_group_by <- function(.data, ...) {   .data |> dplyr::group_by(...) }  # Works with tidy selections too my_select <- function(.data, ...) {   .data |> dplyr::select(...) }  # For single-argument tidy selections, wrap in c() my_pivot_longer <- function(.data, ...) {   .data |> tidyr::pivot_longer(c(...)) }

previous_headings: Modern rlang Patterns for Data-Masking > Function Argument Patterns
title: Modern R Development Guide
text: Use .data pronoun programmatic column access:
code: # Single column by name my_mean <- function(data, var) {   data |> dplyr::summarise(mean = mean(.data[[var]])) }  # Usage - completely insulated from data-masking mtcars |> my_mean("cyl")  # No ambiguity, works like regular function  # Multiple columns with all_of() my_select_vars <- function(data, vars) {   data |> dplyr::select(all_of(vars)) }  mtcars |> my_select_vars(c("cyl", "am"))

previous_headings: Modern rlang Patterns for Data-Masking > Injection Operators
title: Modern R Development Guide
code: # Create symbols from strings var <- "cyl" mtcars |> dplyr::summarise(mean = mean(!!sym(var)))  # Inject values to avoid name collisions df <- data.frame(x = 1:3) x <- 100 df |> dplyr::mutate(scaled = x / !!x)  # Uses both data and env x  # Use data_sym() for tidyeval contexts (more robust) mtcars |> dplyr::summarise(mean = mean(!!data_sym(var)))

previous_headings: Modern rlang Patterns for Data-Masking > Injection Operators
title: Modern R Development Guide
code: # Multiple symbols from character vector vars <- c("cyl", "am") mtcars |> dplyr::group_by(!!!syms(vars))  # Or use data_syms() for tidy contexts mtcars |> dplyr::group_by(!!!data_syms(vars))  # Splice lists of arguments args <- list(na.rm = TRUE, trim = 0.1) mtcars |> dplyr::summarise(mean = mean(cyl, !!!args))

previous_headings: Modern rlang Patterns for Data-Masking > Dynamic Dots Patterns
what: Using list2() for Dynamic Dots Support
title: Modern R Development Guide
code: my_function <- function(...) {   # Collect with list2() instead of list() for dynamic features   dots <- list2(...)   # Process dots... }  # Enables these features: my_function(a = 1, b = 2)           # Normal usage my_function(!!!list(a = 1, b = 2))  # Splice a list my_function("{name}" := value)      # Name injection my_function(a = 1, )               # Trailing commas OK

previous_headings: Modern rlang Patterns for Data-Masking > Dynamic Dots Patterns
title: Modern R Development Guide
code: # Basic name injection name <- "result" list2("{name}" := 1)  # Creates list(result = 1)  # In function arguments with {{ my_mean <- function(data, var) {   data |> dplyr::summarise("mean_{{ var }}" := mean({{ var }})) }  mtcars |> my_mean(cyl)        # Creates column "mean_cyl" mtcars |> my_mean(cyl * am)   # Creates column "mean_cyl * am"  # Allow custom names with englue() my_mean <- function(data, var, name = englue("mean_{{ var }}")) {   data |> dplyr::summarise("{name}" := mean({{ var }})) }  # User can override default mtcars |> my_mean(cyl, name = "cylinder_mean")

previous_headings: Modern rlang Patterns for Data-Masking > Pronouns for Disambiguation
title: Modern R Development Guide
code: # Explicit disambiguation prevents masking issues cyl <- 1000  # Environment variable  mtcars |> dplyr::summarise(   data_cyl = mean(.data$cyl),    # Data frame column   env_cyl = mean(.env$cyl),      # Environment variable   ambiguous = mean(cyl)          # Could be either (usually data wins) )  # Use in loops and programmatic contexts vars <- c("cyl", "am") for (var in vars) {   result <- mtcars |> dplyr::summarise(mean = mean(.data[[var]]))   print(result) }

title: Modern R Development Guide
text: Converting data-masking tidy selection behaviors:
code: # across() as selection-to-data-mask bridge my_group_by <- function(data, vars) {   data |> dplyr::group_by(across({{ vars }})) }  # Works with tidy selection mtcars |> my_group_by(starts_with("c"))  # across(all_of()) as names-to-data-mask bridge   my_group_by <- function(data, vars) {   data |> dplyr::group_by(across(all_of(vars))) }  mtcars |> my_group_by(c("cyl", "am"))

code: # Helps when: ✓ CPU-intensive computations ✓ Embarassingly parallel problems   ✓ Large datasets with independent operations ✓ I/O bound operations (file reading, API calls)  # Hurts when: ✗ Simple, fast operations (overhead > benefit) ✗ Memory-intensive operations (may cause thrashing) ✗ Operations requiring shared state ✗ Small datasets  # Example decision point: expensive_func <- function(x) Sys.sleep(0.1) # 100ms per call fast_func <- function(x) x^2                 # microseconds per call  # Good for parallel map(1:100, in_parallel(expensive_func))  # ~10s -> ~2.5s on 4 cores  # Bad for parallel (overhead > benefit)   map(1:100, in_parallel(fast_func))       # 100μs -> 50ms (500x slower!) # Use vctrs when: ✓ Type safety matters more than raw speed ✓ Building reusable package functions ✓ Complex coercion/combination logic ✓ Consistent behavior across edge cases  # Avoid vctrs when: ✗ One-off scripts where speed matters most ✗ Simple operations where base R is sufficient   ✗ Memory is extremely constrained  # Decision point: simple_combine <- function(x, y) c(x, y)           # Fast, simple robust_combine <- function(x, y) vec_c(x, y)      # Safer, slight overhead  # Use simple for hot loops, robust for package APIs # Use data.table when: ✓ Very large datasets (>1GB) ✓ Complex grouping operations ✓ Reference semantics desired ✓ Maximum performance critical  # Use dplyr when: ✓ Readability and maintainability priority ✓ Complex joins and window functions ✓ Team familiarity with tidyverse ✓ Moderate sized data (<100MB)  # Use base R when: ✓ No dependencies allowed ✓ Simple operations ✓ Teaching/learning contexts

code: library(onet2r)  # Search for occupations onet_search("software developer")  # List occupations (paged) occ <- onet_occupations(start = 1, end = 25)  # Get occupation summary onet_occupation("15-1252.00")  # Retrieve structured details (returns tibbles) onet_skills("15-1252.00", end = 5) onet_knowledge("15-1252.00", end = 5) onet_abilities("15-1252.00", end = 5) onet_tasks("15-1252.00", end = 5)  # Technology endpoints onet_hot_technology("15-1252.00", end = 5) onet_technology_skills("15-1252.00", end = 2)

title: Access O*NET Web Services API data from R
text: typical workflow: search → select → pull details.
code: library(dplyr) library(onet2r)  # Find target occupation target_code <- onet_search("data scientist") |>   filter(title == "Data Scientists") |>   pull(code)  # Pull structured details skills <- onet_skills(target_code, end = 25) tasks  <- onet_tasks(target_code, end = 25) tech   <- onet_hot_technology(target_code, end = 25)  # Pull complete occupation-level sections for downstream analysis all_occupations <- onet_occupations_all(show_progress = FALSE) all_skills <- onet_skills_all(target_code, show_progress = FALSE)  # Combine skills from multiple occupations codes <- c("15-2051.00", "15-1252.00")  all_skills <- codes |>   purrr::map(\(code) onet_skills_all(code, show_progress = FALSE) |> dplyr::mutate(code = code)) |>   purrr::list_rbind()  # Join BLS OEWS national employment and wage estimates oews <- onet_oews_national(2024) wage_context <- all_occupations |>   onet_join_oews(oews = oews)  # If BLS rate-limits automated downloads, download the ZIP manually from # the BLS OEWS public data pages and pass the local file path: oews <- onet_oews_national(2024, path = "oesm24nat.zip")  # Compute employment-weighted skill summaries weighted_skills <- all_skills |>   onet_weighted_summary(     group = c("element_id", "element_name"),     value = "data_value",     oews = oews   )  # Convert ACS/CPS PUMS records to occupation employment weights pums_weights <- onet_pums_employment_weights(pums, socp = "SOCP", weight = "PWGTP")

what: Join O\*NET Occupations to National OEWS Estimates — onet_join_oews
title: Join O\*NET Occupations to National OEWS Estimates — onet_join_oews
text: Joins O\*NET occupation data national OEWS employment wage estimates converting detailed O\*NET-SOC codes "15-1252.00" SOC codes "15-1252".

title: Join O\*NET Occupations to National OEWS Estimates — onet_join_oews
code: onet_join_oews(   occupations,   oews = NULL,   year = latest_oews_year(),   by = "code",   cache_dir = tools::R_user_dir("onet2r", "cache"),   force = FALSE,   quiet = TRUE )

title: Join O\*NET Occupations to National OEWS Estimates — onet_join_oews
text: occupations data frame containing O\*NET occupation code column. oews Optional OEWS tibble. omitted, onet_oews_national() called. year Integer OEWS estimate year, used oews omitted. Name occupation code column occupations. cache_dir Directory used cache downloaded BLS ZIP file. force Logical; TRUE, re-download OEWS data oews omitted. quiet Logical; FALSE, show download progress.

title: Create SOC Employment Weights from PUMS Microdata — onet_pums_employment_weights

title: Create SOC Employment Weights from PUMS Microdata — onet_pums_employment_weights
code: onet_pums_employment_weights(pums, socp = "SOCP", weight = "PWGTP")

title: Create SOC Employment Weights from PUMS Microdata — onet_pums_employment_weights
text: pums data frame containing individual-level occupation records. socp Name occupation-code column. ACS PUMS commonly uses "SOCP". weight Optional name person-weight column. NULL, row receives weight 1. ACS PUMS commonly uses "PWGTP".

title: Create SOC Employment Weights from PUMS Microdata — onet_pums_employment_weights
text: tibble soc_code, employment, records.

title: Create SOC Employment Weights from PUMS Microdata — onet_pums_employment_weights
code: pums <- tibble::tibble(   SOCP = c("151252", "151252", "291141"),   PWGTP = c(120, 80, 200) )  onet_pums_employment_weights(pums) #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   soc_code employment records #>   <chr>         <dbl>   <int> #> 1 15-1252         200       2 #> 2 29-1141         200       1

what: Summarise O\*NET Values with Employment or Wage Weights — onet_weighted_summary
title: Summarise O\*NET Values with Employment or Wage Weights — onet_weighted_summary
text: Computes employment-weighted summaries O\*NET task, skill, ability, work-activity rows joining occupation-level employment wage estimates. useful moving occupation-level O\*NET outputs labor-market-weighted measures.

title: Summarise O\*NET Values with Employment or Wage Weights — onet_weighted_summary
code: onet_weighted_summary(   data,   group,   value,   occupation_code = "code",   oews = NULL,   weight = "tot_emp",   wage = "a_median" )

title: Summarise O\*NET Values with Employment or Wage Weights — onet_weighted_summary
text: data data frame containing one row per occupation-value pair. group Character vector columns group , task skill identifiers. value Name numeric O\*NET value column average. occupation_code Name occupation code column data. oews Optional OEWS tibble. supplied weight already data, joined onet_join_oews(). weight Name employment weight column. Defaults tot_emp, OEWS total-employment column. wage Name wage column used wage-weighted summaries.

title: Summarise O\*NET Values with Employment or Wage Weights — onet_weighted_summary
text: tibble group columns plus n_records, n_occupations, total_weight, weighted_mean, wage_weighted_mean.

title: Summarise O\*NET Values with Employment or Wage Weights — onet_weighted_summary
code: skills <- tibble::tibble(   code = c("15-1252.00", "15-1252.00", "29-1141.00", "29-1141.00"),   element_id = c("2.A.1.a", "2.A.1.b", "2.A.1.a", "2.A.1.b"),   element_name = c(     "Reading Comprehension", "Active Listening",     "Reading Comprehension", "Active Listening"   ),   data_value = c(4.12, 4.00, 3.88, 4.25) )  oews <- tibble::tibble(   occ_code = c("15-1252", "29-1141"),   tot_emp = c(1847900, 3175400),   a_median = c(133080, 93070) )  onet_weighted_summary(   skills,   group = c("element_id", "element_name"),   value = "data_value",   oews = oews ) #> # A tibble: 2 × 7 #>   element_id element_name     n_records n_occupations total_weight weighted_mean #>   <chr>      <chr>                <int>         <int>        <dbl>         <dbl> #> 1 2.A.1.a    Reading Compreh…         2             2      5023300          3.97 #> 2 2.A.1.b    Active Listening         2             2      5023300          4.16 #> # ℹ 1 more variable: wage_weighted_mean <dbl>

title: onet2r 0.1.0
text: onet_cache_use(), onet_cache_clear(), onet_rate_limit() add optional local response caching request spacing repeated bulk O*NET API workflows. onet_join_oews() joins O*NET occupation tibbles national BLS Occupational Employment Wage Statistics (OEWS) employment wage estimates. onet_oews(), onet_oews_national(), onet_oews_state(), onet_oews_metro(), onet_oews_industry() download parse BLS OEWS estimates SOC-level wage employment context. onet_pums_employment_weights() converts ACS/CPS-style PUMS occupation records SOC-level employment weights O*NET joins. onet_weighted_summary() computes employment- wage-weighted summaries O*NET task, skill, ability, work-activity rows. Initial development release O*NET authentication, occupation search, occupation detail helpers, database table access, pagination, military crosswalks, taxonomy mapping.
